request.question: Which American minimalist composer created the symphony Naïve and Sentimental Music, first performed in 1999?

title: John Adams (composer)
Eight Lines
Tromba Lontana
Terry Jennings
Naïve and Sentimental Music
Reed Phase
Akhnaten (opera)
Lollapalooza (Adams)
Tom Johnson (composer)
Bayonne (musician)
sentences: John Coolidge Adams (born February 15, 1947) is an American composer of classical music and opera, with strong roots in minimalism.
Eight Lines, a work by American minimalist composer Steve Reich, was originally titled Octet.
Tromba Lontana (lit.
"distant trumpet") is an orchestral fanfare written by the American minimalist composer John Adams in 1986.
The work was commissioned by the Houston Symphony in commemoration of the 150th anniversary of Texas's declaration of independence from Mexico.
It was given its world premiere by the Houston Symphony under the conductor Sergiu Comissiona in Jones Hall, Houston on April 4, 1986.
The piece contains the voices of two trumpets that are separated from the orchestra, usually performing in the balcony of the concert hall.
Terry Jennings (19 July 1940 – 11 December 1981) was an American minimalist composer and performer.
Naïve and Sentimental Music is a symphonic work by the American composer John Adams.

The title of the work alludes to an essay by Friedrich Schiller, "On Naïve and Sentimental Poetry", that contrasts a creative personality that creates art for its own sake (the "naïve") versus one conscious of other purposes, such as art’s place in history (the "sentimental").
The composer cites both the slowly developing harmonies of Bruckner's Fourth Symphony and the atmosphere of the Sonoma coastline (where the piece was composed) as inspirations for the work.
The piece was co-commissioned by the Los Angeles Philharmonic, the Ensemble Modern, the Vancouver Symphony Orchestra, and the Sydney Symphony Orchestra.
It received its first public performance by the Los Angeles Philharmonic conducted by Esa-Pekka Salonen on February 19, 1999.
A recording by Salonen and the Los Angeles Philharmonic was subsequently released by Nonesuch Records.
Reed Phase, also called Three Reeds, is an early work by the American minimalist composer Steve Reich.

It was written originally in 1966 for soprano saxophone and two soprano saxophones recorded on magnetic tape, titled at that time Saxophone Phase, and was later published in two versions: one for any reed instrument and tape (titled "Reed Phase"), the other for three reed instruments of exactly the same kind (in which case the title is "Three Reeds").
It was Reich's first attempt at applying his "phasing" technique, which he had previously used in the tape pieces "It's Gonna Rain" (1965) and "Come Out" (1966), to live performance.
Akhnaten is an opera in three acts based on the life and religious convictions of the pharaoh Akhenaten (Amenhotep IV), written by the American minimalist composer Philip Glass in 1983.
"Akhnaten" had its world premiere on March 24, 1984, at the Stuttgart State Theatre, under the German title "Echnaton".
Paul Esswood sang the title role, German director Achim Freyer staged the opera in an abstract style with highly ritualistic movements.
The American premiere was held on October 12, 1984, at the Houston Grand Opera, where Glass's opera "The Making of the Representative for Planet 8" also premiered.
Lollapalooza is a short piece composed by American minimalist composer John Adams in 1995.
The piece is based on the rhythm of the word 'Lollapalooza'.
It was composed as a fortieth birthday present to the British conductor Simon Rattle, with whom Adams has worked in the past.
Tom Johnson (born November 18, 1939 in Greeley, Colorado), is an American minimalist composer, a former student of Morton Feldman.
Roger Sellers, also known by his stage name Bayonne, is an American minimalist composer and electronic musician based in Austin, Texas.
After releasing three albums under his own name, Sellers changed his moniker to Bayonne, with the rerelease of his most recent work, Primitives, on Mom + Pop/City Slang in 2016.
Bayonne is known for creating and layering intricate loops as well as his engaging one-man live performances.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Which American minimalist composer created the symphony Naïve and Sentimental Music, first performed in 1999?
hop1_docs: Naïve and Sentimental Music | Naïve and Sentimental Music is a symphonic work by the American composer John Adams.  The title of the work alludes to an essay by Friedrich Schiller, "On Naïve and Sentimental Poetry", that contrasts a creative personality that creates art for its own sake (the "naïve") versus one conscious of other purposes, such as art’s place in history (the "sentimental").  The composer cites both the slowly developing harmonies of Bruckner's Fourth Symphony and the atmosphere of the Sonoma coastline (where the piece was composed) as inspirations for the work.  The piece was co-commissioned by the Los Angeles Philharmonic, the Ensemble Modern, the Vancouver Symphony Orchestra, and the Sydney Symphony Orchestra.  It received its first public performance by the Los Angeles Philharmonic conducted by Esa-Pekka Salonen on February 19, 1999.  A recording by Salonen and the Los Angeles Philharmonic was subsequently released by Nonesuch Records.

Blue cathedral | blue cathedral is an orchestral composition by the American composer Jennifer Higdon.  The work was commissioned by the Curtis Institute of Music in 1999 to commemorate the conservatory's 75th anniversary.  It was first performed in 2000 by the Curtis Institute of Music Symphony Orchestra.  The piece is dedicated to the memory of Higdon's brother and is one of the composer's most performed works.

Symphony No. 5 (Rochberg) | The Symphony No. 5 is the fifth symphony by the American composer George Rochberg.  It was commissioned by the Chicago Symphony Orchestra, which first performed the piece under the direction of Georg Solti on March 13, 1986.  The symphony has a duration of approximately 28 minutes and is cast a single continuous movement.  It was a finalist for the 1986 Pulitzer Prize for Music.

Symphony No. 3 (Price) | The Symphony No. 3 in C minor is a symphony for orchestra by the American composer Florence Price.  The work was commissioned by the Works Progress Administration's Federal Music Project during the height of the Great Depression.  It was first performed at the Detroit Institute of Arts on November 6, 1940, by the Detroit Civic Orchestra under the conductor Valter Poole.  The composition is Price's third symphony, following her Symphony in E minor—the first symphony by a black woman to be performed by a major American orchestra—and her lost Symphony No. 2.

A Sea Symphony | A Sea Symphony is a piece for orchestra and chorus by Ralph Vaughan Williams, written between 1903 and 1909.  Vaughan Williams' first and longest symphony, it was first performed at the Leeds Festival in 1910, with the composer conducting.  The symphony's maturity belies the composer's relative youth when it was written (he was 30 when he first began sketching it).  One of the first symphonies in which a choir is used throughout the work and is an integral part of the musical texture, "A Sea Symphony" helped set the stage for a new era of symphonic and choral music in Britain during the first half of the 20th century.  The work is sometimes referred to as the Symphony No. 1.

Grand Pianola Music | Grand Pianola Music is a minimalist composition by American composer John Adams.  It was written in 1982 and premiered by the San Francisco Symphony in San Francisco's Japan Center.

Evan Ziporyn | Evan Ziporyn (b. Chicago, Illinois, December 14, 1959) is an American composer of post-minimalist music with a cross-cultural orientation, drawing equally from classical music, avant-garde, various world music traditions, and jazz.  Ziporyn has composed for a wide range of ensembles, including symphony orchestras, wind ensembles, many types of chamber groups, and solo works, sometimes involving electronics.  Balinese gamelan, for which he has composed numerous works, has compositions.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Naïve and Sentimental Music — composer John Adams

- "'Naïve and Sentimental Music is a symphonic work by the American composer John Adams.'" (passage 1)
- First public performance: "on February 19, 1999"; performed by the Los Angeles Philharmonic; conductor Esa‑Pekka Salonen. (passage 1)
- Supporting link to minimalism: "Grand Pianola Music is a minimalist composition by American composer John Adams." (passage 6)
- Ambiguity/missing facts: None in the provided passages — the composer identity and 1999 premiere are explicitly stated; no alternative composer candidates for this work appear.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
  1. No additional retrieval needed — passage [1] already identifies composer and 1999 premiere.
  2. (Optional verification) Fetch "John Adams (composer)" — inspect lede/infobox and "Works"/"Compositions" section to confirm genre/complete worklist.
response.hop2_query: John Adams composer Naïve and Sentimental Music works premiere 1999
hop2_docs: Naïve and Sentimental Music | Naïve and Sentimental Music is a symphonic work by the American composer John Adams.  The title of the work alludes to an essay by Friedrich Schiller, "On Naïve and Sentimental Poetry", that contrasts a creative personality that creates art for its own sake (the "naïve") versus one conscious of other purposes, such as art’s place in history (the "sentimental").  The composer cites both the slowly developing harmonies of Bruckner's Fourth Symphony and the atmosphere of the Sonoma coastline (where the piece was composed) as inspirations for the work.  The piece was co-commissioned by the Los Angeles Philharmonic, the Ensemble Modern, the Vancouver Symphony Orchestra, and the Sydney Symphony Orchestra.  It received its first public performance by the Los Angeles Philharmonic conducted by Esa-Pekka Salonen on February 19, 1999.  A recording by Salonen and the Los Angeles Philharmonic was subsequently released by Nonesuch Records.

On Naïve and Sentimental Poetry | On Naïve and Sentimental Poetry ("Über naive und sentimentalische Dichtung") is a 1795 paper by Friedrich Schiller on poetic theory and the different types of poetic relationship to the world.  The work divides poetry into two forms.  Naïve poetry is poetry of direct description while sentimental poetry is self-reflective.  While naïve presents a straight narrative or description, sentimental poetry is built around the authors reflections and relationship to the material.
The Naïve and Sentimental Lover | The Naïve and Sentimental Lover is John le Carré's sixth novel and his only non-genre novel.

Alan Feinberg | Alan Feinberg (born in New York City) is an American classical pianist.  He has premiered over 300 works by such composers as John Adams (composer), Milton Babbitt, John Harbison, Charles Ives, Steve Reich, and Charles Wuorinen, as well as the premiere of Mel Powell's Pulitzer Prize winning "Duplicates".  He is an experienced performer of both classical and contemporary music and is well known for recitals that pair old and new music.

Gnarly Buttons | Gnarly Buttons is a composition for solo clarinet and chamber ensemble by the American composer John Adams.  The London Sinfonietta and Present Music (Milwaukee, Wisconsin) co-commissioned the work.  The work received its premiere at the Queen Elizabeth Hall in London on October 19, 1996 with the London Sinfonietta, Michael Collins as solo clarinetist and the composer conducting.  The work received its first Proms performance on 23 July 1998, conducted by Markus Stenz.  The New York City premiere was in October 1997, with David Shifrin as the clarinet soloist and Ransom Wilson conducting musicians of the Chamber Music Society of Lincoln Center.

On the Transmigration of Souls | On the Transmigration of Souls is a composition for orchestra, chorus, children's choir, and pre-recorded tape by the American composer John Adams (born 1947).  It was commissioned by the New York Philharmonic and Lincoln Center's Great Performers (and an anonymous but prominent New York family) shortly after the September 11 terrorist attacks of 2001.  Adams began writing the piece in late January 2002, and the music was premiered by the New York Philharmonic on September 19, 2002 at Avery Fisher Hall.  The work is composed in a single movement and has a duration of approximately 25 minutes.  Its sheet music is published by Boosey & Hawkes.

Grand Pianola Music | Grand Pianola Music is a minimalist composition by American composer John Adams.  It was written in 1982 and premiered by the San Francisco Symphony in San Francisco's Japan Center.
response.summary_2: Naïve and Sentimental Music is a symphonic work by the American composer John Adams. John Adams is an American composer often associated with minimalism—his 1982 Grand Pianola Music is described as a minimalist composition—and Naïve and Sentimental Music received its first public performance by the Los Angeles Philharmonic under Esa‑Pekka Salonen on February 19, 1999.
